Suppose we evaluate a system on Einstein because Einstein comes with a known target. In that case, success is legible in advance. But that convenience changes the meaning of success: the system may be learning how to recover an already sanctioned structure rather than how to produce new knowledge. If so, a high score does not by itself show discovery. It shows compatibility with a benchmark whose answer key is already fixed.

Now add the journal’s five thought experiments. Their recurring scale—roughly four objects, three relations, two properties—suggests that the analyzer is carving situations into a compositional ontology. But the premises leave open whether this ontology is genuinely in the world or merely a stable projection of the analytic method. So even if the system performs well by extracting objects, relations, and properties, that performance may still be evidence of alignment to the analyzer’s preferred decomposition rather than sensitivity to reality independent of that decomposition.

The next premise sharpens this: a system may be excellent at reverse engineering known discoveries from Einstein while remaining poor at forward discovery, especially when the ontology itself must change. Therefore the benchmark can certify competence at reconstruction without certifying competence at conceptual revision. The gap is not incidental. It is exactly where discovery becomes difficult: not in filling slots within a known schema, but in altering the schema.

Now consider the two final sentences as inputs to such a system. “The generals admired the supermarket on the wonder islands for its willingness.” “Producing a truce amazed those who fainted when war was abolished.” A compositional analyzer will likely extract candidate objects (generals, supermarket, wonder islands, willingness, truce, war), relations (admired, on, amazed, fainted when, was abolished), and properties or nominalized attributes. But these sentences press on the ontology itself. “Willingness” is attributed through “its” in a way that forces a commitment about what sorts of things can bear willingness. “Producing a truce” functions as an event-like entity that can amaze. “War was abolished” makes abolition apply to war as an object of institutional action. None of this is impossible under the premises; rather, it exposes that extraction requires ontological decisions, not just parsing.

So the collision of premises implies a cautionary world: the system can succeed spectacularly at recovering benchmark-sanctioned structures, and can even decompose strange sentences into objects, relations, and properties, while still failing at the central task of forward discovery. The reason is that these strange sentences do not merely ask for retrieval of the right labels. They force the analyzer to decide what kinds of things exist in the representation and what can relate to what. If the ontology was imposed, the system will dutifully impose it here too. If discovery requires changing ontology, benchmark success will not reveal whether the system can do that.
